region_of_origin: Pacific Coast of North America; widely served in Chinese seafood cuisine globally

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a species-level code for Metacarcinus magister; A00LC (Crab, Brachyura) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226808007 covers crab as a food category; no species-specific Dungeness crab code confirmed.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Dungeness crab is approximately 100 g of cooked crab meat, equivalent to roughly one-quarter of a small whole crab.

nutrient_source.title: Crustaceans, crab, dungeness, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15149)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for Dungeness crab, cooked by moist heat, per 100 g edible portion. This is an exact species-level match.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dungeness crab in the University of Sydney GI Database. Shellfish and lean seafood are consistently classified as very low GI (GI ≈ 5) due to negligible carbohydrate content (~0.9 g per 100 g). GI of 5 is assigned by analogy with other cooked crustaceans and lean seafood per the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Glycemic load per 100 g serving is effectively 0.0 given the minimal carbohydrate content.

hypertension.guidance: Dungeness crab contains a moderate amount of sodium (378 mg per 100 g); individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and avoid adding salt or high-sodium sauces such as black bean or XO sauce during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (0.9 g per 100 g) and high lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dungeness crab is an excellent protein source with negligible carbohydrate content, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared without sugar-based or starch-thickened sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, and high selenium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dungeness crab is low in saturated fat and provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and selenium, supporting cardiovascular health; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention in individuals with heart failure or salt-sensitive hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dungeness crab (Metacarcinus magister) is a large Pacific coast crustacean prized for its sweet, delicate, and slightly briny flavour with tender, flaky white meat. It is widely served in Chinese seafood restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, typically steamed whole, stir-fried with ginger and spring onion, or prepared in chilli or black pepper sauce. Per 100 g of cooked meat, Dungeness crab provides approximately 110 kcal, 22.3 g of high-quality protein, and only 1.2 g of fat, making it one of the leanest and most protein-dense seafood options available. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 0.9 g per 100 g. It is an outstanding source of vitamin B12 (9.78 mcg per 100 g, exceeding the adult daily requirement), selenium (40.6 mcg), zinc (4.7 mg), and phosphorus (281 mg). Sodium content is moderate at 378 mg per 100 g from natural sources, which may increase substantially with sauce-based preparations. The glycemic index is effectively zero due to minimal carbohydrate content, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. Clinically, Dungeness crab is a heart-healthy protein choice when prepared with minimal added sodium and fat.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Dungeness crab?
Is Dungeness crab good for diabetics?
Is Dungeness crab high in sodium?
Can I eat Dungeness crab if I have high blood pressure?
Dungeness crab vs mud crab nutrition

How is Dungeness crab prepared in Chinese cuisine?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy exact species-level entry for Dungeness crab cooked by moist heat (NDB 15149). GI is estimated by analogy with lean seafood/crustaceans from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which is the standard approach for near-zero carbohydrate foods. Ontology anchors are defensible with honest match-type declarations.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for Dungeness crab (NDB 15149). Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — naturally zero for this minimally processed crustacean. Sugar_g is 0.0 and dietary_fibre_g is 0.0; both confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central source — naturally zero as crab meat contains no plant-derived carbohydrate structures. GI is set to 5 as an analogous estimate for a near-zero carbohydrate crustacean; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). The input record listed 'Lobster' as a cuisine tag, which is not applicable to this food and has been disregarded. Sodium value (378 mg per 100 g) reflects naturally occurring sodium in cooked crab meat without added salt; restaurant preparations may significantly increase sodium. The food is served at Riverfront Seafood - Boat Quay on field survey data at SGD 9.90 per 100 g portion.
